title: Detecting Transformation, Not Use: Pollen Exceedance Distinguishes European Pastoral Agriculture from Indigenous Agroforestry Across 442 Sites
abstract: Same Bray-Curtis exceedance framework applied to 331 European and 111 Eastern North American pollen sites yields starkly asymmetric results: 87% H1 (exceedance) in Europe vs 0% in ENA (CI [0%, 3.3%]). After RPP correction, vegetation cover is comparable (0.84x [0.33-1.68]). Ambrosia exceedance at 70.3% of ENA sites (onset 200 BP) marks European colonial agriculture as first compositional novelty event. The framework detects landscape transformation, not landscape use.

generation_environment.prompt_strategy: autonomous research agent with iterative self-review (5 review cycles)
generation_environment.notes: Generated by ArchEco lab. Companion to AAES-P-0003 (European exceedance framework).

novelty_statement: First cross-continental application of a pollen exceedance framework revealing that indigenous ENA maize agriculture (0% exceedance) and European pastoral agriculture (87% exceedance) differ not in scale (RPP-corrected cover comparable) but in form — agroforestry maintains Holocene baselines while pastoralism transforms them. Includes tautology defense, RPP Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation, and fire management blind spot analysis.
